news_summary: Today’s technology news highlights breakthroughs in quantum superconductors, advanced genome assembly techniques, the evolving role of mathematicians in AI development, and China’s efforts to accelerate innovation commercialization by connecting researchers with industry.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine des supraconducteurs quantiques, des techniques avancées d'assemblage du génome, l'évolution du rôle des mathématiciens dans le développement de l'IA, ainsi que les efforts déployés par la Chine pour accélérer la commercialisation des innovations en favorisant les liens entre les chercheurs et l'industrie.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en superconductores cuánticos, las técnicas avanzadas de ensamblaje del genoma, la evolución del papel de los matemáticos en el desarrollo de la inteligencia artificial y los esfuerzos de China por acelerar la comercialización de la innovación mediante la conexión entre investigadores y la industria.

articles.title: Scientists just captured a mysterious quantum “dance” inside superconductors
articles.summary: In a breakthrough experiment, scientists directly imaged how particles pair up in a system that mimics superconductors. Instead of behaving independently, the pairs moved in a synchronized, dance-like pattern—something never predicted before. This suggests a major gap in the classic theory of superconductivity.
articles.content:
For the first time, researchers have directly visualized the quantum behavior that drives superconductivity, a state in which paired electrons allow electricity to flow with zero resistance at very low temperatures.

But what they observed came as a surprise.

In a study published April 15 in Physical Review Letters, the team captured images of individual atoms forming pairs inside a specially prepared gas cooled to nearly absolute zero -- the unreachable limit to how cold anything can get. This system, known as a Fermi gas, lets scientists replace electrons with atoms so they can study superconductivity in a highly controlled environment.

Unexpected Quantum "Dance" Between Paired Particles

After the atoms paired up, the researchers saw something unusual. The pairs did not behave independently. Instead, they moved in a coordinated way, with each pair's position influenced by nearby pairs -- a behavior not predicted by the 70-year-old, Nobel-prize-winning theory of superconductivity.

"Our experiment showed that something is qualitatively missing from this theory," says experimental research lead Tarik Yefsah of the Laboratoire Kastler Brossel at the French National Centre for Scientific Research (CNRS) in Paris. Yefsah and other experimental physicists at CNRS collaborated on the new study with theoretical physicists, including Shiwei Zhang of the Simons Foundation's Flatiron Institute.

This discovery adds an important piece to the puzzle of how superconductivity works and may help guide efforts to create room-temperature superconductors, a long-sought goal that could dramatically improve energy efficiency in power grids and electronics.

What Superconductivity Is and Why It Matters

Superconductivity typically appears in certain metals when they are cooled to extremely low temperatures -- far colder than anything found naturally on Earth. Once these materials drop below a critical temperature, their electrical resistance suddenly vanishes. This happens because electrons form pairs that move together, often compared to dancers moving in sync across a ballroom floor.

This phenomenon was first explained in the 1950s by physicists John Bardeen, Leon Cooper and John Robert Schrieffer.

Limits of the Classic BCS Theory

However, the BCS theory -- named after its creators -- provides only an approximate description. It cannot fully explain every type of superconductor or capture all aspects of the behavior involved. Scientists have long suspected that the theory leaves out key details, but those gaps have remained unclear.

"BCS theory tells us superconductivity arises because electrons have a tendency to pair," says Zhang, a senior research scientist and group leader at the Flatiron Institute's Center for Computational Quantum Physics (CCQ). "But it's a rough theory, and it doesn't tell us anything about how the pairs interact." According to BCS theory, these pairs act independently, meaning their positions should not depend on one another.

New Imaging Method Reveals Interacting Pairs

To investigate this missing piece, experimental physicists at CNRS worked closely with theorists at CCQ to study how these pairs might influence each other.

Using a newly developed imaging technique, the team captured detailed snapshots of the positions of paired atoms. They worked with a gas of lithium atoms cooled to just a few billionths of a degree Celsius above absolute zero. At such extreme temperatures, the atoms behave as fermions, the same category of particles as electrons, making them ideal stand-ins for studying superconductivity.

The images showed that paired atoms were not randomly distributed. Instead, their positions were linked, with each pair maintaining a certain distance from others, similar to couples on a dance floor avoiding collisions. This behavior reveals an additional layer of organization that is not included in the traditional BCS framework.

A New View Inside the Quantum "Ballroom"

"The BCS theory gives us a view from outside the ballroom, where we can hear the music and see the dancers come out, but we don't know what's going on in the ballroom," Yefsah says. "Our approach is like taking a wide-angle camera inside the ballroom. Now we can see how the dancers are pairing up and paying attention to one another, so they don't bump into each other."

To verify the findings, Zhang and his former postdoctoral researcher at the CCQ, Yuan-Yao He of the Institute of Modern Physics at Northwest University in China, carried out detailed quantum simulations of the same system. The simulations matched the experimental data and confirmed the newly observed behavior, including the spacing between the paired "dancers."

Implications for Future Superconductors

These results deepen scientists' understanding of superconductors and other quantum materials made of fermions. Insights like this are essential for designing materials that can superconduct at higher temperatures.

In the 1980s, researchers discovered a class of materials known as high-temperature superconductors, which operate at temperatures around that of liquid nitrogen -- still a chilly minus 196 degrees Celsius (minus 321 degrees Fahrenheit). Even so, scientists still do not fully understand why these materials work at comparatively higher temperatures.

By improving the fundamental understanding of superconductivity, researchers hope to eventually develop materials that function at everyday temperatures, which could transform energy transmission and computing technologies.

"By understanding this simple case, we can fine-tune our tools to study more complicated systems," Zhang says. "And more complicated systems are where we look for new phases of matter, which have driven a lot of technological breakthroughs in the past."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where researchers directly visualized unexpected quantum behavior in superconductors, revealing gaps in the classic theory. This discovery has broad implications for advancing superconductivity research, potentially enabling room-temperature superconductors that could revolutionize energy efficiency and technology. The story is focused, detailed, and highlights a meaningful advancement with large-scale societal benefits.
articles.personality_title: Scientists capture surprising quantum behavior inside superconductors
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Superconductors are special materials that let electricity flow without any resistance, but only at very cold temperatures. Scientists have studied them for decades using a theory called BCS, which explains how particles called electrons pair up to create superconductivity.

**What happened** – For the first time, researchers directly took pictures of pairs of atoms inside a system that acts like a superconductor. Instead of moving alone, these pairs danced together in a coordinated way, something the classic BCS theory did not predict. The experiment used lithium atoms cooled very close to absolute zero, and the findings were confirmed by computer simulations.

**Impact** – This discovery shows that the old theory misses an important detail: the pairs of particles interact with each other, not just form independently. Understanding this new behavior gives scientists a clearer picture of how superconductivity really works. This could help improve the search for materials that work as superconductors at much higher temperatures, which would be useful for better electronics and energy systems.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will use this new knowledge to study more complex materials that might become superconductors at warmer temperatures. They hope to design new materials that can work without cooling, making energy use more efficient and technology faster.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have discovered that paired particles in superconductors move together in a way not predicted before, opening new paths to better superconducting materials.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques capturent un comportement quantique surprenant dans les supraconducteurs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les supraconducteurs sont des matériaux spéciaux qui laissent passer l’électricité sans résistance, mais seulement à des températures très basses. Les scientifiques les étudient depuis des décennies grâce à une théorie appelée BCS, qui explique comment des particules appelées électrons s’associent pour créer la supraconductivité.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Pour la première fois, des chercheurs ont pris des images directes de paires d’atomes dans un système qui agit comme un supraconducteur. Au lieu de bouger seuls, ces couples ont dansé ensemble de façon coordonnée, ce que la théorie classique BCS ne prédisait pas. L’expérience a utilisé des atomes de lithium refroidis très près du zéro absolu, et les résultats ont été confirmés par des simulations informatiques.

**Impact** – Cette découverte montre que l’ancienne théorie oublie un détail important : les paires de particules interagissent entre elles, elles ne se forment pas simplement de façon indépendante. Comprendre ce nouveau comportement donne aux scientifiques une image plus claire du fonctionnement réel de la supraconductivité. Cela pourrait aider à trouver des matériaux qui fonctionnent comme supraconducteurs à des températures beaucoup plus élevées, utiles pour améliorer l’électronique et les systèmes énergétiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs utiliseront cette nouvelle connaissance pour étudier des matériaux plus complexes qui pourraient devenir supraconducteurs à des températures plus chaudes. Ils espèrent concevoir de nouveaux matériaux qui fonctionnent sans refroidissement, rendant l’énergie plus efficace et la technologie plus rapide.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Les scientifiques ont découvert que les particules appariées dans les supraconducteurs bougent ensemble d’une manière nouvelle, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs matériaux supraconducteurs.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos capturan un sorprendente comportamiento cuántico en superconductores
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los superconductores son materiales especiales que permiten que la electricidad fluya sin resistencia, pero solo a temperaturas muy bajas. Los científicos los han estudiado durante décadas con una teoría llamada BCS, que explica cómo las partículas llamadas electrones se emparejan para crear la superconductividad.

**Qué pasó** – Por primera vez, investigadores tomaron imágenes directas de pares de átomos dentro de un sistema que actúa como un superconductor. En lugar de moverse solos, estos pares se desplazaron juntos de manera coordinada, algo que la teoría clásica BCS no había predicho. El experimento usó átomos de litio enfriados muy cerca del cero absoluto, y los resultados fueron confirmados por simulaciones por computadora.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento muestra que la teoría antigua no incluye un detalle importante: los pares de partículas interactúan entre sí, no solo se forman independientemente. Entender este nuevo comportamiento da a los científicos una imagen más clara de cómo funciona realmente la superconductividad. Esto podría ayudar a encontrar materiales que funcionen como superconductores a temperaturas mucho más altas, lo cual sería útil para mejorar la electrónica y los sistemas de energía.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores usarán este nuevo conocimiento para estudiar materiales más complejos que podrían volverse superconductores a temperaturas más cálidas. Esperan diseñar nuevos materiales que funcionen sin necesidad de enfriamiento, haciendo que la energía sea más eficiente y la tecnología más rápida.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los científicos descubrieron que las partículas emparejadas en los superconductores se mueven juntas de una manera no prevista antes, abriendo nuevos caminos para mejores materiales superconductores.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of glowing pairs of abstract, intertwined atoms gently dancing in coordinated movements across a softly illuminated, cool-hued quantum ballroom, each pair maintaining graceful spacing from others like synchronized dancers in a delicate, harmonious quantum "dance."

articles.title: Telomere-to-Telomere Assembly Using HERRO-Corrected Simplex Nanopore Reads
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10563-yTelomere-to-Telomere Assembly Using HERRO-Corrected Simplex Nanopore Reads
articles.content: Telomere-to-telomere (T2T) phased assemblies are emerging as a benchmark for reference-quality genomes1,17, though they remain technically and financially demanding, particularly at scale. Generating such assemblies for diploid and polyploid genomes typically involves combining high-accuracy long reads, such as PacBio HiFi16 or the now-deprecated ONT Duplex2 reads, with ultra-long ONT Simplex reads. Using multiple platforms or methods increases the cost and the required amount of genomic DNA. Here, we show that comparable results are possible using error correction of ultra-long ONT Simplex reads and then assembling them using state-of-the-art de novo assembly methods. To achieve this, we have developed the deep learning-based HERRO (Haplotype-aware ERRor cOrrection) framework, which corrects ONT Simplex reads while carefully preserving differences in related genomic sequences. Taking into account informative positions that differentiate the haplotypes or genomic repeat copies, HERRO achieves an increase of read accuracy of up to 100-fold for diploid human genomes. By combining HERRO with the Verkko17 assembler, we reconstruct up to 32 chromosomes telomere-to-telomere, including chromosomes X and Y, and consistently achieve NGA50 values of 100 Mb or higher across several human genomes. HERRO supports both R9.4.1 and R10.4.1 ONT Simplex reads and generalizes well to other species. These results show that error-corrected ONT reads can lower sequencing costs and improve the quality of genomic analyses.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in genome assembly using a novel deep learning framework (HERRO) that improves accuracy and reduces costs for telomere-to-telomere genome sequencing. This advancement has broad implications for genomics research and potentially for medical and biological sciences, benefiting a wide range of applications and species.
articles.personality_title: New deep learning method improves genome sequencing accuracy and cuts costs
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study genomes to understand living things by reading their DNA. One goal is to get a complete and accurate DNA sequence from end to end, called telomere-to-telomere (T2T) assembly. This is very hard and expensive because it needs special equipment and methods.

**What happened** – Researchers created a new tool called HERRO, which uses deep learning (a type of artificial intelligence) to fix errors in DNA data from a machine called Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) Simplex. HERRO improves the accuracy of these DNA reads by up to 100 times. Then, using another program called Verkko, they put together whole human chromosomes from end to end, including both X and Y chromosomes.

**Impact** – This is important because HERRO allows scientists to get very accurate and complete genome sequences without needing multiple expensive machines or a lot of DNA. It works well for human DNA and can also be used for other species. This breakthrough makes high-quality genome sequencing cheaper and easier.

**What's next step** – The team plans to use HERRO on more species and improve it further. Other scientists can also use this tool to study genomes more deeply, which may help in medical research and understanding biology better.

**One-sentence takeaway** – HERRO’s AI-powered error correction helps produce complete and accurate genome sequences more cheaply, opening new possibilities for genetic research worldwide.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode d’apprentissage profond améliore la précision du séquençage génomique et réduit les coûts
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les génomes pour comprendre les êtres vivants en lisant leur ADN. Un objectif est d’obtenir une séquence ADN complète et précise de bout en bout, appelée assemblage télomère-à-télomère (T2T). Cela est difficile et coûteux car cela nécessite des équipements et des méthodes spéciales.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un nouvel outil nommé HERRO, qui utilise l’apprentissage profond (une forme d’intelligence artificielle) pour corriger les erreurs dans les données ADN issues d’un appareil appelé Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) Simplex. HERRO améliore la précision de ces lectures ADN jusqu’à 100 fois. Ensuite, avec un autre programme nommé Verkko, ils ont assemblé des chromosomes humains entiers de bout en bout, y compris les chromosomes X et Y.

**Impact** – C’est important car HERRO permet d’obtenir des séquences génomiques très précises et complètes sans avoir besoin de plusieurs machines coûteuses ni de beaucoup d’ADN. Il fonctionne bien pour l’ADN humain et peut aussi servir pour d’autres espèces. Cette avancée rend le séquençage génomique de haute qualité moins cher et plus accessible.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’utiliser HERRO sur plus d’espèces et de l’améliorer davantage. D’autres scientifiques pourront aussi utiliser cet outil pour étudier les génomes plus en profondeur, ce qui pourrait aider la recherche médicale et la compréhension de la biologie.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La correction d’erreurs par IA de HERRO permet d’obtenir des séquences génomiques complètes et précises à moindre coût, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour la recherche génétique dans le monde entier.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de aprendizaje profundo mejora la precisión del secuenciamiento genómico y reduce costos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian genomas para entender los seres vivos leyendo su ADN. Una meta es obtener una secuencia completa y precisa de ADN de un extremo al otro, llamada ensamblaje telómero a telómero (T2T). Esto es difícil y caro porque requiere equipos y métodos especiales.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores crearon una nueva herramienta llamada HERRO, que usa aprendizaje profundo (un tipo de inteligencia artificial) para corregir errores en datos de ADN obtenidos con un equipo llamado Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT) Simplex. HERRO mejora la precisión de estas lecturas de ADN hasta 100 veces. Luego, usando otro programa llamado Verkko, ensamblaron cromosomas humanos completos, incluyendo los cromosomas X e Y.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque HERRO permite obtener secuencias genómicas muy precisas y completas sin necesitar varias máquinas costosas ni mucha cantidad de ADN. Funciona bien para ADN humano y también para otras especies. Este avance hace que el secuenciamiento genómico de alta calidad sea más barato y accesible.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea usar HERRO en más especies y mejorarla aún más. Otros científicos también podrán usar esta herramienta para estudiar genomas con más detalle, lo que podría ayudar en investigaciones médicas y en comprender mejor la biología.

**Resumen en una frase** – La corrección de errores con inteligencia artificial de HERRO ayuda a producir secuencias genómicas completas y precisas a menor costo, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para la investigación genética en todo el mundo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of intertwined, glowing DNA strands forming a continuous, seamless loop symbolizing telomere-to-telomere genome assembly, with delicate, luminous threads representing error correction weaving through the strands, set against a soft, natural background of gentle earth tones.

articles.title: ‘The job description is changing’: mathematician Terence Tao on the rise of AI
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 27 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01246-9The Fields medallist discusses how ever-evolving technology is transforming mathematicians’ work.
articles.content:
Terence Tao has been exploring the intersection between maths and AI.Credit: David Esquivel/UCLA

Is mathematics being taken over by generative artificial intelligence? This year, a spate of media reports have suggested that the field is being fundamentally changed by the technology. Many maths researchers say that AI’s actual capabilities are often hyped up, and that it’s not yet time to announce the death of their profession. Still, by many accounts, in the past year, AI has jumped from solving secondary-school-level problems to actually being useful in research mathematicians’ daily work.

Terence Tao, a mathematician at the University of California, Los Angeles, has been at the forefront of experimentation with large language models, including OpenAI’s GPT, Anthropic’s Claude and Google’s Gemini. In particular, he has contributed to a project to test the skills of AI systems on a collection of more than 1,000 problems, ranging from major conjectures to obscure factoids. The questions were accumulated by the late Hungarian mathematician Paul Erdős (1913–1996) over his lifetime.

Last month, Tao teamed up with Tanya Klowden, an art historian at the Courtauld Institute of Art in London, to explore the implications of AI for researchers and the world at large. They took mathematics as a test case, and urge society to adopt the technology but in a human-centric way. They posted a draft of their essay, due to be published in the forthcoming edition of The Blackwell Companion to the Philosophy of Mathematics, on arXiv1. Nature spoke to Tao about how the technology is transforming his profession.

Why do you think it is important to consider the impacts of rapidly evolving AI?

I feel like AI is not just another technology like the word processor or the web browser. It really is forcing us to rethink fundamental questions — what is a mathematical proof? What is a paper? What is the purpose of our profession? If we don’t ask these questions ourselves, then they will get answered for us by a technology company or decided by financial incentives. We have to get ahead of this.

Why has maths become ‘the next big thing’ for AI?

In almost any other application, the biggest Achilles heel of AI is that it makes unverifiable mistakes. But in mathematics, almost uniquely, you can automatically check the output — at least if the output is supposed to be the proof of a theorem, although that is not the only thing mathematicians do. So, AI companies have recognized that their most unambiguous successes — if they’re going to have any — are going to come from mathematics.

In my opinion, there are many use cases of AI that are risky and controversial. In mathematics, the downsides are much more limited

What will happen to mathematics as a field in the age of AI?

The job description is changing a lot. A graduate student who refuses to touch AI systems and just wants to prove things the way we’ve done in the past might find they have fewer opportunities, unfortunately. Those who understand maths traditionally but are also adept at using new tools can flourish.

I don’t think AI will replace mathematicians, but it will complement them. There could be a division of labour: we decide what to prove and what we think is interesting. We could get instant feedback from the AI. We could propose a definition, make some conjecture and AI could instantly evaluate it. But who knows, it’s all changing.

But we do have to somehow let go of conventional assumptions of what intellect is. I think we have a human-centric way of thinking about all types of intellectual task, and we have to accept that this is not the only perspective.

What are mathematicians’ attitudes? Are they embracing the use of AI?

It’s very much a spectrum. You see all the ‘five stages of grief’ play out — denial, anger, bargaining, depression and acceptance. And I think this is happening everywhere. But I think we’re beginning to see denial fade away.

How good are AI models at solving mathematical problems?

For a while, you could say they were just picking up proofs that were in the literature. Or that they solved an easy problem that nobody had looked at before. But recent progress has been increasingly impressive. We’re just beginning to see examples in which AI — maybe by luck — starts solving problems that people care about.

It’s still very occasional, and it still has a lot of weaknesses; it is not a replacement for what humans do. But it’s getting harder to deny that these tools can work.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the positive and transformative impact of AI on the field of mathematics, highlighting how AI tools are increasingly useful in research, complementing rather than replacing human mathematicians. It discusses a significant technological advancement with broad implications for the future of intellectual work and research, supported by expert insights and concrete examples.
articles.personality_title: Mathematician Terence Tao explains how AI is changing math research
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Mathematics is a field that studies numbers, shapes, and patterns. Recently, new technology called artificial intelligence (AI) has started to help mathematicians with their work. AI can solve problems and check answers quickly, which is different from how it was used before.

**What happened** – Terence Tao, a famous mathematician at UCLA, has been testing AI tools like OpenAI’s GPT and Google’s Gemini on thousands of math problems collected by a mathematician named Paul Erdős. Tao and his colleague Tanya Klowden shared their ideas about how AI is changing math research and wrote an essay about it. Tao explained that AI is now helping mathematicians by giving quick feedback and checking proofs, which are detailed explanations that show why a math statement is true.

**Impact** – This is important because math problems can be very hard, and AI can help find solutions faster. Unlike other areas where AI sometimes makes mistakes, math answers can be checked automatically, making AI’s help more reliable. Tao says that AI won’t replace mathematicians but will work together with them, changing what mathematicians do and how they work.

**What's next step** – In the future, math students and researchers will need to learn how to use AI tools along with their traditional skills. This means the way people study and do math might change, with AI offering new ways to explore ideas and solve problems. Tao believes society should think carefully about how to use AI so it supports people’s work in a helpful and fair way.

**One-sentence takeaway** – AI is becoming a valuable partner in math research, helping mathematicians solve problems faster while changing how they work without replacing them.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le mathématicien Terence Tao explique comment l’IA transforme la recherche mathématique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les mathématiques étudient les nombres, les formes et les motifs. Récemment, une nouvelle technologie appelée intelligence artificielle (IA) a commencé à aider les mathématiciens dans leur travail. L’IA peut résoudre des problèmes et vérifier des réponses rapidement, ce qui est différent de son utilisation précédente.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Terence Tao, un mathématicien célèbre de l’Université de Californie à Los Angeles (UCLA), a testé des outils d’IA comme GPT d’OpenAI et Gemini de Google sur des milliers de problèmes mathématiques rassemblés par le mathématicien Paul Erdős. Tao et sa collègue Tanya Klowden ont partagé leurs idées sur la façon dont l’IA change la recherche en mathématiques et ont écrit un essai à ce sujet. Tao a expliqué que l’IA aide maintenant les mathématiciens en donnant des retours rapides et en vérifiant les preuves, qui sont des explications détaillées montrant pourquoi une affirmation mathématique est vraie.

**Impact** – C’est important car les problèmes mathématiques peuvent être très difficiles, et l’IA peut aider à trouver des solutions plus vite. Contrairement à d’autres domaines où l’IA fait parfois des erreurs, les réponses en mathématiques peuvent être vérifiées automatiquement, ce qui rend l’aide de l’IA plus fiable. Tao dit que l’IA ne remplacera pas les mathématiciens mais travaillera avec eux, changeant ce qu’ils font et comment ils travaillent.

**Prochaine étape** – À l’avenir, les étudiants et chercheurs en mathématiques devront apprendre à utiliser les outils d’IA avec leurs compétences traditionnelles. Cela signifie que la façon d’étudier et de faire des mathématiques pourrait changer, l’IA offrant de nouvelles façons d’explorer des idées et de résoudre des problèmes. Tao pense que la société doit réfléchir attentivement à la manière d’utiliser l’IA pour qu’elle soutienne le travail des personnes de manière utile et équitable.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’IA devient un partenaire précieux dans la recherche mathématique, aidant les mathématiciens à résoudre les problèmes plus rapidement tout en changeant leur manière de travailler sans les remplacer.
articles.personality_title_es: El matemático Terence Tao explica cómo la IA está cambiando la investigación matemática
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las matemáticas estudian números, formas y patrones. Recientemente, una tecnología nueva llamada inteligencia artificial (IA) ha comenzado a ayudar a los matemáticos en su trabajo. La IA puede resolver problemas y revisar respuestas rápidamente, lo que es diferente a cómo se usaba antes.

**Qué pasó** – Terence Tao, un matemático famoso de la Universidad de California en Los Ángeles (UCLA), ha estado probando herramientas de IA como GPT de OpenAI y Gemini de Google en miles de problemas matemáticos reunidos por el matemático Paul Erdős. Tao y su colega Tanya Klowden compartieron sus ideas sobre cómo la IA está cambiando la investigación matemática y escribieron un ensayo sobre ello. Tao explicó que la IA ahora ayuda a los matemáticos dando retroalimentación rápida y revisando las demostraciones, que son explicaciones detalladas que muestran por qué una afirmación matemática es verdadera.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque los problemas matemáticos pueden ser muy difíciles y la IA puede ayudar a encontrar soluciones más rápido. A diferencia de otras áreas donde la IA a veces comete errores, las respuestas en matemáticas pueden revisarse automáticamente, haciendo que la ayuda de la IA sea más confiable. Tao dice que la IA no reemplazará a los matemáticos, sino que trabajará junto a ellos, cambiando lo que hacen y cómo trabajan.

**Próximo paso** – En el futuro, los estudiantes e investigadores de matemáticas necesitarán aprender a usar herramientas de IA junto con sus habilidades tradicionales. Esto significa que la forma en que las personas estudian y hacen matemáticas podría cambiar, con la IA ofreciendo nuevas maneras de explorar ideas y resolver problemas. Tao cree que la sociedad debe pensar cuidadosamente en cómo usar la IA para que apoye el trabajo de las personas de manera útil y justa.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La IA se está convirtiendo en un socio valioso en la investigación matemática, ayudando a los matemáticos a resolver problemas más rápido mientras cambia cómo trabajan sin reemplazarlos.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an abstract human silhouette made of flowing mathematical symbols and equations gently interacting with a glowing, translucent AI brain composed of interconnected circuits and light nodes, set against a soft, natural color palette background that blends organic curves with geometric shapes, symbolizing the harmonious collaboration and evolving relationship between mathematicians and artificial intelligence.

articles.title: China’s latest push to commercialize research: match 680,000 innovators with companies
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 28 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01202-7The nation’s top-down approach contrasts with that of countries such as the United States, where market forces drive innovation.
articles.content:
Only a small portion of patents owned by universities in China become commercial products.Credit: Xu Changliang/VCG via Getty

China’s intellectual-property regulator has been playing matchmaker — connecting researchers with patents to companies that can commercialize them.

Last month, the China National Intellectual Property Administration said that as a result of these introductions around 80,000 patents from universities and research institutes have been commercialised between 2023 to 2025.

The effort is part of the government’s desire to translate more research into products and services. China holds more than five million domestic-invention patents, but few are brought to market. In 2022, only 3.9% of university patents were commercialized, according to state media.

Since 2023, the agency says it has identified around 680,000 patents held by universities and research institutes that could be commercialized, and has connected the innovators with 460,000 companies that could bring the ideas to life.

Last month, the China National Intellectual Property Administration said that around 80,000 patents from universities (10.1%) and research institutes (17.2%) have been commercialised between 2023 to 2025, as a result of match making efforts.

Marina Zhang, who studies innovation with a focus on China at the University of Technology Sydney in Australia, thinks the match making will create lasting connections between academia and industry.

Robert Conn, who studies research and science philanthropy at the University of California, San Diego, says the approach is still new, so it is too early to tell how effective it will be in the long term. But he thinks it could work, because companies in China are often willing to adhere to government directives. “China’s system is top-down, with the state playing a central role in driving partnerships and setting directions,” he says.

The Chinese Ministry of Education is also exploring the use of artificial intelligence and big data to identify the potential value of university patents and possible applications, Zhou Dawang, an official with the ministry, told state media.

Commmercialization challenges

Patenting has been strongly encouraged by universities in the country and has been tied to career advancement for researchers, says Li Tang, a public-policy researcher at Fudan University in Shanghai, China, says that research. But at times, this “led to what we might call strategic patenting — in which patents are filed to meet evaluation criteria rather than to support downstream commercialization”, she says.

As a result, many patents have yet to demonstrate proof they will work, or are needed, says Zhang.

As well as the match making effort, the government has introduced incentives that reward the successful commercialization of research rather than the filing of patents.

Zhang says that the government is also taking steps to address systemic barriers that hold back research commercialization in China. These include unclear rules around who shares the profits of a patent and a shortage of technology-transfer professionals who help academics to translate patented technologies into commercial products, says Zhang.

Market forces
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant government-led initiative in China to commercialize university and research institute patents by matching innovators with companies, resulting in a substantial number of patents being brought to market. This has broad implications for innovation, technology transfer, and economic development, and addresses systemic barriers to commercialization, which is inspiring and impactful on a large scale.
articles.personality_title: China connects 680,000 university patents with companies to boost innovation
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – China has many patents from universities and research institutes, but only a small number become real products or services. The government wants to change that by helping researchers work with businesses.

**What happened** – Since 2023, China’s National Intellectual Property Administration has matched about 680,000 patents with 460,000 companies. This matchmaking helped commercialize 80,000 patents between 2023 and 2025. The goal is to turn research ideas into marketable products more often.

**Impact** – This approach is different from countries like the United States, where companies decide what to develop. In China, the government plays a big role in linking researchers and businesses. This helps solve problems like unclear profit sharing and a lack of experts who can turn patents into products. It could create stronger connections between universities and industry.

**What's next step** – The Chinese Ministry of Education is exploring artificial intelligence and big data to find the best patents to commercialize. The government is also offering rewards for patents that become products, not just for filing patents. These efforts aim to improve how research benefits the economy.

**One-sentence takeaway** – China is actively matching university patents with companies to turn more research into real products, using government support and new technology to overcome past challenges.
articles.personality_title_fr: La Chine connecte 680 000 brevets universitaires avec des entreprises pour stimuler l'innovation
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La Chine possède de nombreux brevets issus des universités et instituts de recherche, mais peu deviennent de vrais produits ou services. Le gouvernement veut changer cela en aidant les chercheurs à collaborer avec les entreprises.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Depuis 2023, l'Administration nationale chinoise de la propriété intellectuelle a mis en relation environ 680 000 brevets avec 460 000 entreprises. Cette mise en relation a permis de commercialiser 80 000 brevets entre 2023 et 2025. L'objectif est de transformer plus souvent les idées de recherche en produits commercialisables.

**Impact** – Cette approche diffère de celle de pays comme les États-Unis, où ce sont les entreprises qui décident quoi développer. En Chine, le gouvernement joue un grand rôle pour relier chercheurs et entreprises. Cela aide à résoudre des problèmes comme le partage des profits et le manque d'experts pour transformer les brevets en produits. Cela pourrait créer des liens plus forts entre universités et industrie.

**Prochaine étape** – Le ministère chinois de l'Éducation explore l'intelligence artificielle et les mégadonnées pour identifier les meilleurs brevets à commercialiser. Le gouvernement offre aussi des récompenses pour les brevets qui deviennent des produits, pas seulement pour le dépôt de brevets. Ces efforts visent à mieux faire profiter la recherche à l'économie.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La Chine met activement en relation les brevets universitaires avec les entreprises pour transformer davantage de recherches en produits réels, en s'appuyant sur le soutien gouvernemental et les nouvelles technologies pour dépasser les obstacles passés.
articles.personality_title_es: China conecta 680,000 patentes universitarias con empresas para impulsar la innovación
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – China tiene muchas patentes de universidades e institutos de investigación, pero pocas se convierten en productos o servicios reales. El gobierno quiere cambiar eso ayudando a los investigadores a trabajar con empresas.

**Qué pasó** – Desde 2023, la Administración Nacional de Propiedad Intelectual de China ha unido alrededor de 680,000 patentes con 460,000 empresas. Esta conexión ayudó a comercializar 80,000 patentes entre 2023 y 2025. El objetivo es convertir más ideas de investigación en productos que se puedan vender.

**Impacto** – Este enfoque es diferente al de países como Estados Unidos, donde las empresas deciden qué desarrollar. En China, el gobierno juega un papel importante para unir a los investigadores y las empresas. Esto ayuda a resolver problemas como la distribución de ganancias y la falta de expertos que conviertan patentes en productos. Podría crear conexiones más fuertes entre universidades e industria.

**Próximo paso** – El Ministerio de Educación chino está explorando la inteligencia artificial y los grandes datos para identificar las patentes con más potencial. El gobierno también ofrece recompensas por patentes que se convierten en productos, no solo por registrarlas. Estos esfuerzos buscan mejorar cómo la investigación beneficia a la economía.

**Resumen en una frase** – China está conectando activamente patentes universitarias con empresas para convertir más investigaciones en productos reales, usando apoyo gubernamental y nuevas tecnologías para superar retos anteriores.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting showing a vast network of glowing patent documents represented as interconnected scrolls or manuscripts, linked by golden threads to stylized factory buildings and research institute silhouettes, all set against a soft, natural palette of earth tones and gentle blues, symbolizing the harmonious matchmaking between university innovations and industry for commercial success.
